questions.question: When should you use an HPA (scale replicas horizontally) vs a VPA (increase pod resources vertically) for a Kubernetes workload?
options: HPA is for stateless apps; VPA is for stateful apps that cannot be replicated.

HPA is ideal for stateless, scalable workloads (web APIs, workers) where more replicas = more capacity. VPA is better for single-instance workloads (databases, singleton services) where adding more pods doesn't help but giving the existing pod more CPU/memory does.
HPA scales based on HTTP requests; VPA scales based on CPU only.
HPA requires the Metrics Server; VPA requires Prometheus.
questions.answer: HPA is ideal for stateless, scalable workloads (web APIs, workers) where more replicas = more capacity. VPA is better for single-instance workloads (databases, singleton services) where adding more pods doesn't help but giving the existing pod more CPU/memory does.
questions.explanation: You can run 50 identical API pods simultaneously. But you cannot easily run 50 independent PostgreSQL primary pods — you need one big pod. HPA adds copies; VPA makes existing copies bigger.

questions.question: What specific advantage does KEDA provide over standard HPA for a Kafka consumer deployment?
options: KEDA can scale down to zero pods when there is no work — standard HPA cannot.
Both KEDA and HPA can scale Kafka consumers based on CPU utilization.
KEDA only works with Kafka; standard HPA works with all workload types.
KEDA scales based on pod restart counts; HPA scales based on CPU.
questions.answer: KEDA can scale down to zero pods when there is no work — standard HPA cannot.
questions.explanation: HPA has a minimum of 1 replica (you always pay for at least one running pod). KEDA can scale to 0 replicas when the Kafka consumer lag is 0, then instantly scale back up when messages arrive, eliminating idle compute costs entirely.

questions.question: What is the HPA's primary scaling formula, and what does it tell you about how the autoscaler behaves when CPU spikes suddenly?
options: HPA adds one replica per extra percentage point of CPU above the threshold.

HPA calculates desired replicas as: ceil(currentReplicas × (currentCPU / targetCPU)). If you have 4 pods at 120% of target CPU, it computes ceil(4 × 1.2) = 5 new pods needed. This proportional scaling means a 2x spike in load triggers approximately 2x the replicas.
HPA always doubles the replica count when any metric exceeds its threshold.
HPA adds replicas one at a time with a 5-minute cooldown between each addition.
questions.answer: HPA calculates desired replicas as: ceil(currentReplicas × (currentCPU / targetCPU)). If you have 4 pods at 120% of target CPU, it computes ceil(4 × 1.2) = 5 new pods needed. This proportional scaling means a 2x spike in load triggers approximately 2x the replicas.
questions.explanation: This proportional formula means HPA responds gracefully to gradual load increases and aggressively to sudden spikes — no manual tuning needed. It's fundamentally a control loop that continuously adjusts to maintain the target metric.
